sections.heading: What you're seeing
sections.body: High-level usage stats for the selected period: sessions, prompts, tokens, cache efficiency, and estimated API cost.

sections.heading: Daily / Hourly Token Usage
sections.body: Stacked bar chart showing output tokens, non-cached input, cache reads, and cache creation over time. When period is "Day", shows hourly breakdown.

sections.heading: Token Breakdown
sections.body: Doughnut chart of total token distribution across the four categories: output, input, cache read, and cache creation.

sections.heading: Cache Usage
sections.body: Shows how effectively prompt caching is working. Higher cache read % means less redundant token processing and lower cost.

sections.heading: Cumulative API Value vs Plan Fee
sections.body: Tracks how much API value you've consumed over time against your monthly plan fee. If the line exceeds the dashed plan fee line, you're getting more than 1× value from your subscription.

sections.heading: Tokens by Model
sections.body: Stacked bar showing input and output token consumption per model. Helps you see which models you use most heavily.

sections.heading: Stop Reasons
sections.body: How often each stop reason occurs. "end_turn" means the model finished naturally, "tool_use" means it called a tool, "max_tokens" means it hit the output limit.

sections.heading: Top Projects
sections.body: Horizontal bar chart of your most token-intensive projects. Useful for understanding where your usage is concentrated.

sections.heading: Sessions by Entrypoint
sections.body: Pie chart showing how sessions are distributed across entrypoints (CLI, VS Code extension, API, etc.).

sections.heading: 5-Hour Usage Windows
sections.body: Claude tracks usage in rolling 5-hour windows. Each bar shows the API value consumed in that window. Red bars indicate windows where throttling occurred.

sections.heading: Top Conversations by API Cost
sections.body: Your most expensive individual conversations. Hover for details including prompt count, dominant model, and percentage of plan fee.

sections.heading: What this tab measures
sections.body: Compares the API-equivalent value of your Claude usage against your subscription plan cost. Helps answer: “Should I upgrade or downgrade?”

sections.heading: Plan Verdict
sections.body: “Good Value” means your API-equivalent usage exceeds your plan fee (you’re getting more than you pay). “Underusing” means you’re paying more than the equivalent API cost.

sections.heading: Suggested Plan
sections.body: Based on your average weekly API-equivalent cost, extrapolated to monthly. Compares against Pro ($20), Team Standard ($25), Max 5x ($100), Team Premium ($125), and Max 20x ($200) tiers.

sections.heading: Weekly Activity
sections.body: Bar chart showing sessions and prompts per week. Helps you see your usage patterns and whether your activity level justifies your current plan.

sections.heading: API Cost per 5-Hour Window
sections.body: Shows absolute API-equivalent cost per 5-hour window. Bars are orange for windows that contained an assistant response cut off at the output-token limit (a "truncated output"). Note: Anthropic's 5-hour rate-limit rejections never reach the session logs, so we cannot reliably show a real "% of limit" value.

sections.heading: Windows Per Week
sections.body: Stacked bar showing total 5-hour usage windows per week. The orange segment counts windows that contained a truncated-output response — a non-critical signal that usually points at a single long response being cut off at the output-token limit.

sections.heading: Multi-Account Support
sections.body: If you use multiple Claude accounts (e.g. work team + personal), each account is detected separately with its own plan verdict and cost breakdown.

sections.heading: What this tab measures
sections.body: Analyzes your context management habits: conversation length, context window growth, compaction usage, and cache efficiency. Helps you answer: "Am I managing my context optimally?"

sections.heading: Conversation Length Distribution
sections.body: Histogram of how many prompts your sessions contain. Very long sessions (15+ prompts) without compaction can lead to higher costs and slower responses as the context window fills up.

sections.heading: Context Growth Curve
sections.body: Shows how input tokens grow with each successive prompt in a conversation. A steep curve means context is accumulating fast — consider using /compact or starting new conversations for unrelated tasks.

sections.heading: Cache Efficiency by Length
sections.body: Compares cache hit rates across short vs long conversations. If cache efficiency drops in longer sessions, it may indicate the context is being rebuilt too often or /compact is discarding useful cached context.

sections.heading: Compaction Events
sections.body: Detected instances where input tokens dropped significantly between messages (>40% reduction), indicating /compact was used. Shows the before/after token counts to visualize how much context was reclaimed.

sections.heading: Long Sessions Table
sections.body: Sessions with 15+ prompts, sorted by peak input tokens. The “Compacted?” column shows whether compaction was detected. Sessions marked “No” may have benefited from running /compact to reduce context size and cost.

sections.heading: What this tab measures
sections.body: Analyzes whether you're sending prompts to a more expensive model than necessary. Each user prompt ("turn") is scored for complexity, then classified into a recommended tier: Haiku, Sonnet, or Opus.

sections.heading: Potential Savings
sections.body: Dollar amount you could save if every turn had been routed to the cheapest model capable of handling it. Only counts cases where you used a more expensive model than the classified tier.

sections.heading: Overuse Rate
sections.body: Percentage of classified turns where the actual model was more expensive than the recommended tier. A high overuse rate suggests opportunities to use cheaper models for simple tasks.

sections.heading: Model Usage by Complexity Tier
sections.body: Stacked bar per model, colored by classified tier (green=Haiku, blue=Sonnet, red=Opus). If Opus has a large green or blue segment, those turns could have used a cheaper model.

sections.heading: Opus Complexity Score Distribution
sections.body:
Histogram of complexity scores (0–100) for turns that were sent to Opus. Scores are computed from four signals:

• Output tokens (0–25 pts) — longer responses indicate harder tasks
• Thinking blocks (0–30 pts) — extended thinking is the strongest complexity signal
• Tool complexity (0–25 pts) — Agent, Write, NotebookEdit score highest
• Prompt text analysis (0–20 pts) — keywords like "refactor" or "architect" add points; "fix typo" or "rename" subtract

Bars are colored by tier: green (0–20, Haiku-level), blue (20–40, Sonnet-level), red (40+, Opus-level). Green and blue bars represent Opus turns that may not have needed Opus.

sections.heading: Top Overuse
sections.body: The specific turns with the highest potential savings — cases where an expensive model was used for a task classified as simpler. Shows a preview of the prompt text and the dollar savings if the tier-appropriate model had been used.

sections.heading: What this tab measures
sections.body: Estimates the electricity and CO₂ footprint of your Claude usage. Computed per-message using model-class energy coefficients (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus) and your detected or locale-inferred grid region. Uncertainty is ±55% — Anthropic does not publish per-request energy data.

sections.heading: Grid Region
sections.body: Energy-to-CO₂ conversion uses the carbon intensity of the detected inference region (from inference_geo on each message). Falls back to your locale if no geo is detected. You can see coverage % and which regions were seen.

sections.heading: Cache Savings
sections.body: Cache read tokens are ~3% the energy cost of re-computing them as input tokens. This section shows estimated energy and CO₂ saved by prompt caching.

sections.heading: Environmental Equivalents
sections.body: Converts total energy and CO₂ to intuitive units: liters of gasoline burned (2.31 kgCO₂/L), area of solar panels that would take the selected period to generate the same energy in the detected grid region (~20% efficiency, 0.75 performance ratio), wind-turbine rotations (~1.17 kWh per rotation on a 3 MW onshore turbine at 35 % capacity factor), engineered nuclear-repository volume (canisters + buffer + tunnel excavation, weighted across HLW+ILW+LLW, counterfactual 100%-nuclear alternative, ~4 mL/kWh), km travelled by local transit (30 gCO₂/pkm, bus + metro + tram blend) and long-distance electrified train (6 gCO₂/pkm). A journey anchor snaps the period's driven-km-equivalent to a recognizable trip. Useful for communicating footprint in concrete terms.

sections.heading: What you can configure
sections.body: Plan type (Pro, Max, Team, Custom) and monthly fee. These are used for plan utilization calculations on the Plan tab. If not set, the tool auto-detects from telemetry.

sections.heading: Cost Alert Thresholds
sections.body: Set daily, weekly, and monthly cost thresholds. When estimated API cost exceeds a threshold, alerts are shown after collection.

sections.heading: Where settings are stored
sections.body: All settings are saved to ~/.claude-stats/config.json. You can also edit this file directly.
